---
title: "ICP and Herniation Management"
---

12. IV fluids: 0.9% normal saline to maintain euvolemia
13. NPO for angiogram OR bedside swallow study and diet, formal swallow study if needed
14. Continue necessary home medications; hold all antithrombotic medications
15. Avoid Valsalva maneuver: use antiemetics, antitussives and stool softeners
16. Pain management: scheduled Tylenol/acetaminophen 1g q6h if no liver disease. Codeine 30-60 mg q 6
hours PRN OR Ultram (tramadol) 25-50 mg q 6 hours PRN IF patient does not have seizures
17. In case of seizures: Keppra (levetiracetam). (try to avoid fosphenytoin - poorer outcomes)
18. DVT prophylaxis: SCDs, no heparin
19. GI prophylaxis: PPI (e.g., pantoprazole, omeprazole or lansoprazole [if gtube])
20. Consult PMR/Speech therapy as indicated
21. Code status
Modified Fisher Scale for World Federation of Hunt and Hess
vasospasm prediction Neurological Surgeons
0=no subarachnoid blood I=GCS 15, no motor deficit 0=unruptured aneurysm
1=thin focal or diffuse SAH; no II=GCS 13-14, no motor deficit 1=asymptomatic or mild HA and
intraventricular blood nuchal rigidity
2=thin focal or diffuse SAH; III=GCS 13-14, motor deficit 1a=no meningeal signs but have
intraventricular extension Present fixed neurologic deficit
3=thick focal or diffuse SAH; no IV=GCS 7-12, motor deficit 2= CN palsy, mod/severe HA,
intraventricular blood present or absent nuchal rigidity
4=thick focal or diffuse SAH; V=GCS 3-6, motor deficit 3=mild focal deficit, confusion or
intraventricular extension present or absent drowsiness
4=stupor, mod/severe hemiparesis
5=deep coma, decerebrate
posturing, moribund appearance
**For Hunt and Hess - add one grade for severe vasospasm on angiogram or presence of serious systemic disease
(HTN, DM, COPD, severe atherosclerosis)
Events/complications after aneurysmal SAH
Early Late Chronic
Rebleeding Cerebral vasospasm Chronic hydrocephalus that may
require placement of a shunt
Acute hydrocephalus Delayed cerebral ischemia Behavior, personality, memory
changes
Seizures Pituitary dysfunction Need for further retreatment for
recurrent or residual anerusym
Cardiopulmonary issues and stress Infection related to surgery or
induced cardiomyopathy ventriculostomy
Sodium and electrolyte
abnormalities
Acute elevations in ICP and management of elevated ICP:
Sx of elevated ICP include:
Non-focal: nausea, vomiting, hiccups, increased yawning, agitation,
headache, diminished level of consciousness, Cushing’s
response (elevated BP, bradycardia, respiratory depression),
If any concern for worsening or impending herniation: Clinical
suspicion or sustained (>5 min) elevation of ICP > 22 mmHg
• Neurocritical care fellow: 352-745-1912
• Ensure appropriate vascular access, consider need for
additional PIV and/or stat central line, transfer to ICU if
on floor/IMC


Herniation syndromes
Subfalcine • Infarct in distal ACA territory  ipsilateral cingulate gyrus migrates under anterior falx
 ipsilateral leg weakness
Central • Ascending: compression of PCA and SCA against tentorium
• Descending: progression of abnormal flexor posturing to extensor posturing  impending
death due to damaged respiratory and cardiac centers in medulla by herniated cerebellar
tonsils through foramen magnum
Cerebellar tonsillar • PICA, vertebral artery, anterior spinal artery infracts  infarct to brainstem, tonsils, and
lower cerebellum, upper cervical cord  respiratory arrest, downbeat nystagmus
Uncal • Compression of 3rd nerve against tentorial edge  constriction followed by ipsilateral
pupillary dilation
• Compression of calcarine branch of PCA  infarct in temporal or occipital lobe
• Compression of sylvian aqueduct  obstructive hydrocephalus
• Midbrain displacement can cause compression on contralateral cerebral peduncle /
contralateral corticospinal tract  ipsilateral hemiparesis to compression (Kernohan’s
notch phenomenon)
Transalar (trans- • Descending / posterior variant: infarct in MCA territory from compression within
sphenoidal) sphenoid ridge
• Ascending / anterior variant: infarct in anterior and middle cerebral arteries from
compression of supra-clinoid segment of ICA against anterior clinoid process
Transcalvarial • Often after decompressive hemicraniectomy  compression of cortical vessels along
bony margin predisposing to hemorrhagic infarct
Management of herniation
• Assess ABCs
• Minimize noxious stimuli (treat subclinical seizures, glucose control, treat shivering)
• Analgesia / sedation (control pain and agitation at the lowest effective doses (fentanyl,
lorazepam, propofol)
• Target normothermia
Tier 0
• Head of bed ≥ 30º (unless unstable MAP or brain sag)
(Standard)
• Neck midline (optimize jugular venous drainage)
• Iso or hyperosmotic fluids (NO D5W, ½ NS, etc)
• Avoid and correct hyponatremia
• Steroids in select conditions (brain tumor, abscess, non-infectious neuroinflammatory
conditions)
⇩
• Hyperosmolar therapy
• Temporary hyperventilation to a PaCO2 of 30-35 mmHg, consider 10 cc 1% lidocaine into
Tier 1 ETT prior to suctioning
• CSF drainage
• Consider surgical decompression
⇩
• Hyperosmolar therapy for higher Na goals
Tier 2 • Optimize sedation and analgesia
• Rescue decompressive surgery should be considered as a life saving intervention
⇩
• Sedation titrated to ICP goal or burst suppression of cEEG (pentobarbital coma)
• Hyperventilation to achieve mild to moderate hypocapnia (PaCO2 25-34 mmHg), short
lived, <24 hours, works by ↑ vasoconstriction and ↓ cerebral blood volume. Effects at the
Tier 3
expense of ↓ CBF therefore used only as bridge to urgent surgical therapy. Not
recommended for routine management. Can be bridge to OR in actively herniating patients
• Moderate hypothermia (target core temperature 32-34ºC)


Hyperosmolar therapy
Dosing Adverse Reactions Pearls
• Give 20% 1g/kg bolus; max 150g • Rebound ICP elevation • Relative contraindications: renal
• Can be re-dosed every 4-6 hrs, consider re-dose if: with abrupt failure/insufficiency
Mannitol • Posm <320 and ICP still >20 discontinuation / repeat • May be given via peripheral
(0.5-1 g/kg • Posm >320, calculate osmolar gap (measured Posm – [2 x plasma Na dosing • Duration of effect 90 min – 6 hrs
over 5-15 + Glc/18 + BUN/2.8]); if < 20 AND Na <160, give addition 1 g/kg • Acute kidney injury • Monitor trough osmolar gap (goal < 20
mins) • Repeat until ICP <20 or definitive therapy • Hypovolemia mOsm/kg)
• Hypotension • Maintain euvolemia – replete urine output
• Electrolyte imbalances cc for cc with normal saline
Bolus dosing • Pulmonary edema • Central access needed for >2% gtt or
• 2% or 3%: 250 ml over 30 min • Heart failure 23.4% bolus
• 23.4%: 30 ml over 30 min q6h • Acute kidney injury • Duration of effect 90 min–4 h
Hypertonic
Continuous infusions • Coagulopathy • Avoid prolonged hypernatremia > 160
Saline (goal
• 2% or 3% to maintain Na<155 or Posm<320 • Hypernatremia • Decrease chloride and increase acetate
145-155)
• Na should not rise >8-10 pts in 24 hrs • Metabolic acidosis content if patients develop metabolic
Normal saline (154 mmol/L) • Thrombophlebitis acidosis
Target Na
• Baseline hyponatremia (Na<131 mmol/L) • Osmotic demyelination • Can be prepared as a 1:1 NaCl:NaAcetate
(q2-4h):
2% gtt (342 mmol/L): monitor Na q2-4h syndrome with rapid solution if hyperchloremic metabolic
145-155
• Mild-moderate edema or baseline sodium close to target (~140 and correction acidosis
mmol/L
above; patient should NOT be hyponatremic (<130)) • If persistent hypotension with bradycardia
• Peripheral or central line after bolus  norepi gtt
Target
• Initiation: 0.5-1 ml/kg/hr • Have nurse repeat all Na samples with
osmolality
• Infusion rate not exceed 125 ml/hr dramatic changes as they frequently are
(q4-12h):
3% gtt (513 mmol/L): monitor Na q2-4h inaccurate
300-320
• Severe edema or baseline sodium far from target (131-140) • If too quick drop during discontinuation,
mOsmol/L
• Central line restart previous solution at prior rate
or higher as
indicated
• Initiation: 0.5 ml/kg/hr with/without a 25 – 50% decrease
• Peripheral up to 48 hours at rate <50 cc/hr – discuss with NCC first
• Rebound edema can occur on d/c, observe in ICU ~24 hours
Discontinuation: Decrease rate by 50% every 4-6 hours, once rate is 20
ml/hr, it can be stopped


![](images/icp_and_herniation_management_page19.png)
